original_abstract: Porous metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have long been overlooked for electronic device applications, primarily due to the scarcity of frameworks that exhibit electrical conductivity. In this work, we demonstrate that conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs represent a powerful new class of materials for the chemiresistive sensing of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). We show that a sensor array constructed from a family of structurally analogous but chemically distinct 2D MOFs enables the clear discrimination of various VOC categories with high accuracy. Experimental evidence suggests that these materials operate through multiple, orthogonal sensing mechanisms—including charge transfer and potentially hydrogen bonding—distinguishing them from traditional chemiresistor materials. These results establish conductive MOFs as a highly tunable and mechanistically unique platform for next-generation sensory technologies.
humanized_abstract: Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have rarely been investigated as porous materials for electronic device applications due to their scarcity and lack of electric conductivity. In this work, we show that the newly-emerging family of conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs could be a novel class of chemiresistive materials for the detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). The sensor array based on a small set of structurally similar yet chemically diverse 2D MOFs is capable of successfully and selectively distinguishing and identifying multiple categories of VOCs with great accuracy. Our findings indicate that the newly-fabricated materials detect the presence of different VOC molecules via multiple orthogonal sensing routes, such as charge transfer that is unique to this family of MOF materials, while excluding other surface interactions such as hydrogen and van der Waals forces commonly present in most chemiresistor-based sensors. Therefore, conductive MOFs could offer a flexible yet chemically distinctive sensing platform for potential use in future sensor devices.

Our findings indicate that the newly-fabricated materials detect the presence of different VOC molecules via multiple orthogonal sensing routes, such as charge transfer that is unique to this family of MOF materials, while excluding other surface interactions such as hydrogen and van der Waals forces commonly present in most chemiresistor-based sensors. Therefore, conductive MOFs could offer a flexible yet chemically distinctive sensing platform for potential use in future sensor devices.
